title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Chile — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Espresso is king
items.text: Chileans drink small, strong espressos (café or cafecito), not large milky coffees. If you want milk, ask for café con leche, but expect a tiny cup of espresso topped with hot milk, not a latte.

items.label: Café culture is social, not rushed
items.text: Cafés are gathering places for long conversations. Sitting at a table costs slightly more than standing at the counter, but you're welcome to stay for hours with one coffee. This is normal and expected.

items.label: Seek out traditional corner cafés
items.text: Skip chains in favor of small neighborhood cafés (cafeterías) where locals gather. You'll find better coffee, lower prices, and authentic interaction—look for places with older regulars and worn wooden counters.

items.label: Completo for breakfast
items.text: Pair your coffee with a completo—a loaded hot dog with avocado, tomato, mayonnaise, and mustard. It's the standard Chilean breakfast/snack and costs under 2,000 pesos.

items.label: Lunch (almuerzo) is the main meal
items.text: Most restaurants offer a fixed-price lunch menu (menú del día) from noon to 2pm with an appetizer, main, drink, and dessert for 8,000-15,000 pesos. This is the best value—dinner menus are more expensive and à la carte.

items.label: Understand the course structure
items.text: A full meal includes entrada (appetizer/starter), plato de fondo (main), and postre (dessert). Many restaurants expect you to order all three, though you can ask for just a main course.

items.label: Seafood is seasonal and regional
items.text: Availability depends on location and season. Coastal towns have fresh fish year-round, while inland restaurants may have limited options. Ask what's fresh today (¿qué es lo más fresco?) rather than assuming based on the menu.

items.label: Prices rarely include service
items.text: Menu prices are before tax and service. Expect to pay 10% propina (tip) separately, though it's not automatically added like in other countries. Check your bill carefully.

items.label: Watch for tourist-trap translations
items.text: In tourist areas, beware of menus with English translations featuring inflated prices or Chileanisms mistranslated (like 'empanada' becoming 'Chilean pastry'). Stick to restaurants where locals eat.

items.label: Pastel de choclo
items.text: A baked corn pie layered with ground beef, chicken, olives, and hard-boiled egg, topped with fresh corn purée. It's comfort food and appears on most menus—order it at a traditional restaurant, not a tourist-focused one.

items.label: Ceviche
items.text: Raw fish or seafood marinated in lime juice, served with onions, cilantro, and avocado. Chile's Pacific coast versions are exceptional; order it for lunch, never dinner, and only in coastal regions where it's made fresh daily.

items.label: Cazuela
items.text: A hearty stew traditionally made with beef, chicken, or seafood, served in a clay bowl with potatoes, corn, and pumpkin in broth. It's served at lunch and is deeply Chilean—nothing fancy, just authentic comfort.

items.label: Empanadas
items.text: Fried or baked pastries filled with meat (pino), cheese (queso), or seafood. Street vendors and bakeries sell them fresh; empanadas de pino are the most common and cost under 1,500 pesos.

items.label: Congrio (conger eel)
items.text: A meaty white fish unique to Chilean waters, often simply grilled or prepared in caldillo de congrio (a soupy fish stew). It's expensive but worth trying once at a quality seafood restaurant.

items.label: Curanto
items.text: A southern specialty (especially Chiloé) of seafood, meat, and potatoes cooked in a pit or pot. It's labor-intensive and best eaten where it originates—order it in the south, not in Santiago restaurants.

items.label: Local neighborhood restaurants (picadas)
items.text: Look for small, unpretentious spots where workers eat lunch—these serve authentic food at low prices with zero tourist markup. They're often unmarked or have handwritten menus and no English spoken, which is a good sign.

items.label: Avoid restaurant clusters in major tourist zones
items.text: In Santiago's Lastarria or Bellavista neighborhoods, restaurants are overpriced and mediocre. Walk two blocks away from the main street to find family-run places serving better food for half the price.

items.label: Markets (ferias) for casual eating
items.text: Every neighborhood has a feria (market) with food stalls serving fresh seafood, prepared dishes, and juices at minimal cost. La Vega in Santiago and local markets in coastal towns are excellent for lunch.

items.label: Timing matters for lunch
items.text: Arrive between 12:30 and 1:30pm for the menú del día (fixed lunch). After 2pm, most restaurants stop serving lunch specials and switch to à la carte pricing, which is significantly more expensive.

items.label: Parrillas for grilled meat
items.text: Parrillas (grillhouses) offer excellent value for meat lovers, with set menus featuring chargrilled beef, chicken, and sausages. These are casual, lively places and popular with locals—quality is usually consistent.

items.label: Chilean wine is exceptional value
items.text: Wine is cheap and excellent—a good bottle costs 8,000-15,000 pesos in restaurants, less in shops. Red wines from Central Valley and white wines from coastal regions are world-class. Never feel obligated to order expensive bottles.

items.label: Wine with meals is normal and affordable
items.text: A glass of wine costs 3,000-5,000 pesos with lunch; a bottle for two people is often cheaper than individual glasses. Wine pairing with meals is expected and encouraged, not a luxury.

items.label: Pisco sour
items.text: Chile's national spirit cocktail made with pisco (grape brandy), lime juice, egg white, and syrup. Pisco comes from northern Chile; order it at bars and restaurants, not from the street. A quality pisco sour is strong and balanced, not a sugary tourist drink.

items.label: Bottled water or tap water
items.text: Tap water is safe throughout Chile. Restaurants always serve bottled water (agua mineral) unless you specify tap water (agua de la llave or agua simple). Bottled water costs 1,000-3,000 pesos; requesting tap water is normal and not rude.

items.label: Chicha
items.text: A traditional fermented drink made from grapes or apples, especially common in September (Independence Day). It's sweet, slightly fizzy, and low alcohol—buy it from street vendors or markets during spring/early summer.

items.label: Juice (jugos) culture
items.text: Fresh-squeezed fruit and vegetable juices are everywhere and incredibly cheap (2,000-4,000 pesos). Order by asking for the fruit (naranja for orange, fresa for strawberry) and specify natural (sin azúcar) if you don't want added sugar.

items.label: Dinner is late and leisurely
items.text: Chileans eat dinner (cena) between 8pm and 10pm. Restaurants don't get busy until 9pm, and arriving early makes you stand out as a tourist. Meals are social events lasting 1-2 hours—rushing is considered rude.

items.label: Bread and butter are complimentary but charged
items.text: Restaurants automatically bring bread and butter; if you eat it, you'll be charged 1,000-2,000 pesos. Push it away if you don't want to pay, or ask 'sin pan, por favor' when ordering.

items.label: Tip appropriately but not excessively
items.text: 10% propina is standard for sit-down meals; less for casual spots. Tip in cash if possible, or tell the server the amount to add to the card. Tipping 20% marks you as a wealthy tourist and inflates prices for others.

items.label: Don't expect large portions
items.text: Chilean portions are modest by North American standards. If hungry, order an extra side dish (papas fritas, arroz) or start with an appetizer. Doggy bags aren't standard; restaurants may look confused if you ask.

items.label: Respect meal timing and restaurant hours
items.text: Lunch closes at 2pm sharp; dinner service starts at 7-8pm. Many restaurants close between lunch and dinner or on Sundays/Mondays. Planning ahead and calling ahead, especially outside major cities, prevents disappointment.

items.label: Be polite and patient with language barriers
items.text: Learning basic Spanish phrases (por favor, gracias, ¿cuál es la especialidad?) goes far and is appreciated. Most service staff in tourist areas speak some English, but rural areas don't—patience and pointing at menus works.
